elements.sent-2411-001: Jie Chen \IEEEauthorblockA{College of Computer Science Sichuan University Chengdu, China chenjie2010@scu.edu.cn \and \IEEEauthorblockN{2\textsuperscript{rd Hua Mao \IEEEauthorblockA{Department of Computer and Information Sciences Northumbria University Newcastle upon Tyne, U.K.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: Graph contrastive learning (GCL) has shown promising performance in semisupervised graph classification.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, existing studies still encounter significant challenges in GCL.
elements.sent-2411-004: First, successive layers in graph neural network (GNN) tend to produce more similar node embeddings, while GCL aims to increase the dissimilarity between negative pairs of node embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-005: This inevitably results in a conflict between the message-passing mechanism of GNNs and the contrastive learning of negative pairs via intraviews.
elements.sent-2411-006: Second, leveraging the diversity and quantity of data provided by graph-structured data augmentations while preserving intrinsic semantic information is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-007: In this paper, we propose a self-supervised conditional distribution learning (SSCDL) method designed to learn graph representations from graph-structured data for semisupervised graph classification.
elements.sent-2411-008: Specifically, we present an end-to-end graph representation learning model to align the conditional distributions of weakly and strongly augmented features over the original features.
elements.sent-2411-009: This alignment effectively reduces the risk of disrupting intrinsic semantic information through graph-structured data augmentation.
elements.sent-2411-010: To avoid conflict between the message-passing mechanism and contrastive learning of negative pairs, positive pairs of node representations are retained for measuring the similarity between the original features and the corresponding weakly augmented features.
elements.sent-2411-011: Extensive experiments with several benchmark graph datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed SSCDL method.
elements.sent-2411-012: Graph-structured data typically represent non-Euclidean data structures, which are often visualized as graphs with labels \cite{Kazemi2020RL .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The analysis of such data has attracted considerable attention in various applications, e.g., social network recommendation \cite{Fan2022GNNF , molecule classification \cite{Wang2022MCL , and traffic flow analysis \cite{Li2021STFG .}}}
elements.sent-2411-014: In graph classification tasks, graph-structured data typically consists of multiple graphs, each with its own independent topology.
elements.sent-2411-015: In practice, obtaining large-scale labelled annotations for graph-structured data is often prohibitively costly or even impossible.
elements.sent-2411-016: Semisupervised graph classification aims to predict the labels of graphs via a small number of labeled graphs and many unlabeled graphs.
elements.sent-2411-017: Numerous graph neural networks (GNNs) have been proposed for the analysis of graph-structured data, e.g., the graph convolutional network (GCN) \cite{Kipf2017GCN , graph attention network (GAT) \cite{Veli2018GAT , graph sampling and aggregation (GraphSAGE) \cite{Hamilton2017SAGE and their variants.}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Through the message-passing mechanism, GNNs aggregate semantic information from neighboring nodes in the graph topology to central nodes.
elements.sent-2411-019: In graph classification tasks, GNNs aims to transform individual graphs into corresponding low-dimensional vector representations at the graph level \cite{Yue2022SSGC .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Consequently, GNNs are capable of integrating graph-structured data into comprehensive graph representations, preserving the intrinsic structure of graph-structured data \cite{Vignac2020GNN .}
elements.sent-2411-021: Contrastive learning, which employs raw image data as its own supervision, has shown great potential in computer vision \cite{Chen2024SEF, Chen2023DMC, Chen2020CL, Oord2018RL .}
elements.sent-2411-022: Several augmentation operations for visual data, such as rotating, cropping, and flipping, significantly improve the representation ability of visual learning models in downstream tasks \cite{Tian2020GV .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Wang et al. \cite{Wang2023CL proposed a general contrastive learning framework that minimizes the distribution divergence between weakly and strongly augmented images over a representation bank.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Inspired by the previous success of visual data augmentation, graph contrastive learning (GCL) leverages the advantages of graph data augmentation to maximize the similarity between positive pairs and the dissimilarity between negative pairs \cite{Wu2021SSL .}
elements.sent-2411-025: For example, You et al. proposed a graph contrastive learning (GraphCL) framework that employs four types of augmentations to learn invariant graph representations across different augmented views, including node dropping, edge perturbation, attribute masking and subgraph extraction \cite{You2020GCL .}
elements.sent-2411-026: Zhu et al. proposed a graph contrastive representation learning method with adaptive augmentation that incorporates various priors for topological and semantic information of the graph \cite{Zhu2021CGL .}
elements.sent-2411-027: These GCL-based approaches effectively enhance the generalizability of graph representation learning models in a self-supervised manner.
elements.sent-2411-028: GCL for graph classification has raised significant interests recently \cite{Ju2024SSGC, Han2022GDA, Yue2022SSGC .}
elements.sent-2411-029: For example, Han et al. \cite{Han2022GDA adopted $\mathcal{G}$-Mixup to augment graphs for graph classification.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Yue et al. \cite{Yue2022SSGC proposed a graph label-invariant augmentation (GLIA) method that adds perturbations to the node embeddings of graphs for creating augmented graphs.}
elements.sent-2411-031: However, these approaches have overlooked two limitations in GCL.
elements.sent-2411-032: First, successive GNN layers tend to make node representations more similar due to the message-passing mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-033: In contrast, GCL aims to enhance the dissimilarity of negative pairs of node embeddings through mutual information maximization.
elements.sent-2411-034: This inevitably results in a potential conflict between message-passing mechanism of GNNs and the contrastive learning of negative pairs in intra-views.
elements.sent-2411-035: Second, different augmentations for graph-structured data often result in varying performances for GNNs \cite{Yue2022SSGC .}
elements.sent-2411-036: For example, heavy edge perturbation can enhance the diversity and quantity of graph-structured data.
elements.sent-2411-037: Unfortunately, it may also disrupt the intrinsic semantic information contained in the graph-structured data.
elements.sent-2411-038: Therefore, the other significant challenge is how to leverage the diversity and quantity provided by graph-structured data augmentation while preserving the intrinsic semantic information.
elements.sent-2411-039: In this paper, we propose a self-supervised conditional distribution learning (SSCDL) method for learning graph representations from graph-structured data for semisupervised graph classification.
elements.sent-2411-040: We define slight perturbations to node attributes or graph topology as weak augmentations, whereas significant perturbations are referred to as strong augmentations.
elements.sent-2411-041: We first present an end-to-end graph representation learning model to align the conditional distributions of weakly and strongly augmented features over the original features.
elements.sent-2411-042: Aligning the conditional distributions effectively reduces the risk of compromising intrinsic semantic information when strong augmentation is applied.
elements.sent-2411-043: Moreover, only positive pairs of node representations are retained for measuring the similarity between the original features and their corresponding weakly augmented features.
elements.sent-2411-044: From the gradient perspective of the InfoNCE loss, we explain how the conflict between the message-passing mechanism of GNNs and the contrastive learning of negative pairs of node representations can be avoided.
elements.sent-2411-045: Additionally, we introduce a semisupervised learning scheme that includes a pretraining stage and a fine-tuning stage for semisupervised graph classification.
elements.sent-2411-046: Extensive experimental results with benchmark graph datasets demonstrate that the proposed SSCDL method is highly competitive with state-of-the-art graph classification methods.
elements.sent-2411-047: Our major contributions are summarized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-048: We present an end-to-end graph representation learning model that learns graph representations from graph-structured data for semisupervised graph classification.
elements.sent-2411-049: Self-supervised conditional distribution learning is introduced to characterize the consistent conditional distributions of weakly and strongly augmented node embeddings over the original node embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-050: The similarity loss function is introduced to alleviate the potential conflict between the message-passing mechanism of the GNN and the contrastive learning of negative pairs in GCL.

elements.section-2411-004: Self-Supervised Conditional Distribution Learning on Multiple Graphs
elements.sent-2411-075: In this section, we formally introduce the proposed SSCDL method, including the SSCDL model and a semisupervised learning scheme for graph classification.
elements.sent-2411-076: Fig. \ref{fig:architecture presents the framework of the SSCDL model, which consists of three modules: a GNN encoder module for processing graph-structured data, a projection head module and a self-supervised distribution construction module.}
elements.sent-2411-077: The shared GNN encoder module learns graph-level representations, $\mathbf{H}$, $\mathbf{H _s$ and $\mathbf{H _w$, from the raw graph-structured data, a strongly augmented view and a weakly augmented view, respectively.}}
elements.sent-2411-078: These graph-level representations are obtained from the node embeddings of the graphs through a graph pooling layer.
elements.sent-2411-079: In particular, $\mathbf{H}$ are used for semisupervised graph classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-080: The projection head module is designed to produce the projected representations of the raw graph-structured data augmentation and a weakly augmented view for GCL.
elements.sent-2411-081: The self-supervised conditional distribution module constructs the conditional distributions of strongly and weakly embedded features, using the original embeddings as a reference.
elements.sent-2411-082: When working with relatively small amounts of graph-structured data, GNN-based models often learn redundant features.
elements.sent-2411-083: Graph-structured data augmentation aims to increase the diversity and quantity of graph-structured data, which can improve the generalizability and robustness of GCL-based models.
elements.sent-2411-084: By applying strong augmentation, GNN-based models can learn essential features from graph-structured data, even when these data are slightly corrupted by noise.
elements.sent-2411-085: However, strong augmentations, such as extensive edge perturbation or attribute masking, can also significant perturb the original data, potentially altering the intrinsic semantic information contained in the graph-structured data and thus resulting in limited generalizability of GNN-based models.
elements.sent-2411-086: To minimize the risk of altering intrinsic semantic information with strong augmentation, we introduce a self-supervised conditional distribution learning strategy to guide graph representation learning.
elements.sent-2411-087: This learning strategy explores the supervision information embedded in the original graph-structured data for weak and strong augmentations.
elements.sent-2411-088: We consider the distribution of relative similarity between a weakly augmented node embedding and the original node embedding.

elements.sent-2411-101: To evaluate the similarity between a positive pair $\left( {\mathbf{h _i^w, {\mathbf{h _i \right)$, the similarity loss is formulated as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:losssim} \begin{split} {\mathcal{L}_s} = - \frac{1}{{{n_g}}}\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{{n_g}} {\log \frac{{\exp \left( {sim\left( {\mathbf{h}_i^w,{\mathbf{h}_i}} \right)} \right)}}{{\sum\limits_{j \ne i}^{n_g} {\exp \left( {sim\left( {\mathbf{h}_i^w,{\mathbf{h}_j}} \right)} \right)} }}}. \end{split}\end{equation} Given a pair of negative node embeddings $\left( {\mathbf{h _i^w,{\mathbf{h _j \right)$ $\left(j \ne i\right)$ in Eq. \eqref{eq:losssim , $\mathbf{h _i^w$ does not participate in the message-passing of the SSCDL model involving with ${\mathbf{h _j}$, as these embeddings originate from a different augmented view.}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-102: In this section, we introduce a semisupervised training scheme for graph classification.
elements.sent-2411-103: This training scheme consists of two stages: a pretraining stage and a fine-tuning stage.
elements.sent-2411-104: When many unlabeled graphs are available, an intuitively designed pretraining framework is an effective approach for semisupervised graph classification.
elements.sent-2411-105: The pretraining framework of the SSCDL model includes a shared GNN encoder module of graph-structured data and a projection head module.
elements.sent-2411-106: The shared GNN encoder module consists of three components, including a GNN encoder component, a pooling layer component and a multilayer perceptron (MLP) component.
elements.sent-2411-107: In the shared GNN encoder module, we perform graph representation learning according to graph classification theory.
elements.sent-2411-108: For simplicity, a GCN \cite{You2020GCL, Kipf2017GCN is used as an example to describe the backbone of the GNN encoder module.}
elements.sent-2411-109: The formula for the $l$th GCN layer in the GNN encoder is defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:gcn} \begin{split} {\mathbf{H}^{(l)}} = \sigma \left( {{{\widetilde {\mathbf{D}}}^{ - {1 \mathord{\left/ {\vphantom {1 2}} \right. \kern-\nulldelimiterspace} 2}}}{\widetilde {\mathbf{A}}}{{\widetilde {\mathbf{D}}}^{ - {1 \mathord{\left/ {\vphantom {1 2}} \right. \kern-\nulldelimiterspace} 2}}}{\mathbf{H}^{(l - 1)}}{\mathbf{W}^{(l)}}} \right) \end{split}\end{equation} where ${\mathbf{W ^{(l) }$ is a weight matrix of the $l$th layer, ${\mathbf{H ^{(l) }$ represents a node-level embedding matrix with $\mathbf{H ^{(0) =\mathbf{X}$, and $\sigma$ is a nonlinear activation function, e.g., ${\rm ReLU}\left( \cdot \right) = \max \left( {0, \cdot \right)$.}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-110: Then, we employ a global sum pooling layer as a READOUT function to obtain graph-level representations from the node-level embeddings, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{eq:gcn} \begin{split} {\mathbf{H}_\mathcal{G}} = pooling\left( {\mathbf{H}^{(L)}} \right). \end{split}\end{equation} Finally, the MLP component is composed of several fully connected layers, with computations defined as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:fmlp} \begin{split} \mathbf{H}= \mathbf{MLP}\left( {\mathbf{H}_\mathcal{G},{\mathbf{W}^{(l)}}} \right), \end{split}\end{equation} where a nonlinear activation function, ${\rm ReLU}\left( \cdot \right)$, is applied for each linear layer.
elements.sent-2411-111: To improve the generalizability of the SSCDL model, we apply a batch normalization function in individual layers \cite{Ioffe2015BN .}
elements.sent-2411-112: During the pretraining stage, the graph-level representations, $\mathbf{H}$ and $\mathbf{H _w$, are produced by the shared GNN encoder module via the raw graph-structured data and a weakly augmented view, respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-113: We further employ the projection head module \cite{Chen2020CL , which consists of a two-layer MLP, to obtain the projections of $\mathbf{H}$ and $\mathbf{H _w$ for contrastive learning, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{eq:proj} \begin{split} \mathbf{P} = {\mathbf{W}^{\left( 2 \right)}}\sigma\left( {{\mathbf{W}^{\left( 1 \right)}}\mathbf{H}} \right) \mathbf{P}_w = {\mathbf{W}^{\left( 2 \right)}}\sigma\left( {{\mathbf{W}^{\left( 1 \right)}}\mathbf{H}_w} \right). \end{split}\end{equation} The pretraining framework of the SSCDL model aims to ensure the consistency of the intrinsic semantic information between a weakly augmented view and the original graph-structured data.}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Therefore, the loss function in Eq. \eqref{eq:losssim is employed during the pretraining stage.}
elements.algorithm-2411-001: \renewcommand{\algorithmicrequire}{Input:} \renewcommand\algorithmicensure {Output: } \caption{Optimization Procedure for SSCDL} \label{alg:sscdl} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \REQUIRE ~~ The training graphs $\mathcal{G}_r = \left\{ {\left( {\mathcal{G}_i} \right)} \right\}_{i = 1}^{{n_g}}$, the testing graphs $\mathcal{G}_t = \left\{ {\left( {\mathcal{G}_i} \right)} \right\}_{i = 1}^{{n_t}}$, the number of iterations $epochs$, parameters $\alpha > 0 $ and $\beta > 0$. \end{algorithmic} \begin{algorithmic}[1] \STATE Construct a weakly augmented view $\mathcal{G}_s$ and a strongly augmented view $\mathcal{G}_w$ from $\mathcal{G}_r$; \FOR{ ${t = 1}$ to $epochs$ } \STATE Compute ${\mathbf{P}}$ and ${\mathbf{P}_w}$ via Eq. \eqref{eq:proj} using the unlabeled parts of $\mathcal{G}_r$ and $\mathcal{G}_w$, respectively; \STATE Update the network by minimizing ${\mathcal{L}_{s}}$ in Eq. \eqref{eq:losssim} \ENDFOR \FOR{ ${t = 1}$ to $epochs$ } \STATE Compute $\mathbf{p}_i$ via Eq. \eqref{eq:sm} for each labeled graph in $\mathcal{G}_r$; \STATE Compute ${\mathbf{P}}$ and ${\mathbf{P}_w}$ via Eq. \eqref{eq:proj} using the labeled parts of $\mathcal{G}_r$ and $\mathcal{G}_w$, respectively; \STATE Compute $p\left( {\mathbf{h}_i^w|{\mathbf{h}_i}} \right)$ and $p\left( {\mathbf{h}_i^s|{\mathbf{h}_i}} \right)$ via Eq. \eqref{eq:ff} using the labeled parts of $\mathcal{G}_r$, $\mathcal{G}_s$ and $\mathcal{G}_w$; \STATE Update the network by minimizing ${\mathcal{L}}$ in Eq. \eqref{eq:ovloss} \ENDFOR \STATE Compute $\mathbf{p}_i$ via Eq. \eqref{eq:sm} for each testing graph in $\mathcal{G}_t$; \ENSURE The predicted labels of the testing graphs $\mathcal{G}_t$. \end{algorithmic}
elements.sent-2411-115: During the fine-tuning stage, a small number of labeled graphs are employed to train the SSCDL model.

elements.sent-2411-205: In this paper, we introduce the SSCDL method for semisupervised graph classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-206: The SSCDL model is designed to align the conditional distributions of weakly and strongly augmented features over the original features.
elements.sent-2411-207: The proposed SSCDL method leverages the diversity and abundance provided by graph-structured data augmentations while preserving intrinsic semantic information.
elements.sent-2411-208: Moreover, SSCDL resolves the conflict between the message-passing mechanism of GNNs and the contrastive learning of negative pairs within intraviews.
elements.sent-2411-209: Additionally, we present a semisupervised learning scheme consisting of pretraining and fine-tuning stages.
elements.sent-2411-210: Extensive experimental results with various graph datasets demonstrate the superior performance of the SSCDL method.
elements.sent-2411-211: Jie~Chen \IEEEauthorblockA{College of Computer Science Sichuan University Chengdu, China chenjie2010@scu.edu.cn \and \IEEEauthorblockN{2\textsuperscript{rd Hua~Mao \IEEEauthorblockA{Department of Computer and Information Sciences Northumbria University Newcastle upon Tyne, U.K.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-212: Leveraging the diversity and quantity of data provided by various graph-structured data augmentations while preserving intrinsic semantic information is challenging.
elements.sent-2411-213: Additionally, successive layers in graph neural network (GNN) tend to produce more similar node embeddings, while graph contrastive learning aims to increase the dissimilarity between negative pairs of node embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-214: This inevitably results in a conflict between the message-passing mechanism (MPM) of GNNs and the contrastive learning (CL) of negative pairs via intraviews.
elements.sent-2411-215: In this paper, we propose a conditional distribution learning (CDL) method that learns graph representations from graph-structured data for semisupervised graph classification.
elements.sent-2411-216: This alignment enables the CDL model to effectively preserve intrinsic semantic information when both weak and strong augmentations are applied to graph-structured data.
elements.sent-2411-217: To avoid the conflict between the MPM and the CL of negative pairs, positive pairs of node representations are retained for measuring the similarity between the original features and the corresponding weakly augmented features.
elements.sent-2411-218: Extensive experiments with several benchmark graph datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed CDL method.
elements.sent-2411-219: In graph classification tasks, graph-structured data consists of multiple graphs, each with its own independent topology.
elements.sent-2411-220: Numerous graph neural networks (GNNs) have been proposed for the analysis of graph-structured data, e.g., the graph convolutional network (GCN) \cite{Kipf2017GCN , graph attention network (GAT) \cite{Veli2018GAT and their variants.}}
elements.sent-2411-221: For example, You et al. \cite{You2020GCL proposed a GCL framework that employs four types of augmentations to learn invariant graph representations across different augmented views, including node dropping, edge perturbation, attribute masking and subgraph extraction.}
elements.sent-2411-222: Zhu et al. \cite{Zhu2021CGL proposed a graph contrastive representation learning method with adaptive augmentation that incorporates various priors for topological and semantic information of the graph.}
elements.sent-2411-223: These GCL-based approaches effectively enhance the generalizability of graph representation learning models.
elements.sent-2411-224: First, different augmentations for graph-structured data often result in varying performances for GNNs \cite{Yue2022SSGC .}
elements.sent-2411-225: For example, heavy edge perturbation often disrupts the intrinsic semantic information contained in the graph-structured data.
elements.sent-2411-226: Therefore, a significant challenge is how to leverage the diversity and quantity provided by various graph-structured data augmentations while preserving the intrinsic semantic information.
elements.sent-2411-227: Second, successive GNN layers tend to make node representations more similar due to the message-passing mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-228: This inevitably results in a potential conflict between message-passing mechanism of GNNs and the contrastive learning of negative pairs in intraviews.
elements.sent-2411-229: Aligning the conditional distributions effectively reduces the risk of disrupting intrinsic semantic information when strong augmentation is applied to the graph-structured data.
elements.sent-2411-230: From the perspective of the mutual information loss, we explain how the conflict between the message-passing mechanism of GNNs and the contrastive learning of negative pairs of node representations can be avoided.
elements.sent-2411-231: Extensive experimental results with benchmark graph datasets demonstrate that the proposed CDL method is highly competitive with state-of-the-art graph classification methods.
elements.sent-2411-232: Conditional distribution learning is introduced to characterize the consistent conditional distributions of weakly and strongly augmented node embeddings over the original node embeddings.

elements.section-2411-007: Conditional Distribution Learning on Multiple Graphs
elements.sent-2411-243: In this section, we formally introduce the proposed CDL method, including the CDL model and a semisupervised learning scheme for graph classification.
elements.sent-2411-244: Fig. \ref{fig:architecture shows the framework of the CDL model, which consists of three modules: a shared GNN encoder module, a projection head module and a conditional distribution construction module.}
elements.sent-2411-245: In particular, $\mathbf{H}$ is used for semisupervised graph classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-246: The conditional distribution construction module constructs the conditional distributions of strongly and weakly augmented node embeddings given the original node embeddings.
elements.sent-2411-247: When dealing with relatively small amounts of graph-structured data, GNN-based models often learn redundant features due to the message-passing and aggregation mechanisms.
elements.sent-2411-248: To effectively learn essential features from graph-structured data, GCL-based models apply strong augmentation to graph-structured data.
elements.sent-2411-249: However, strong augmentations, such as edge perturbation or attribute masking, often significantly perturb the original graph-structured data, potentially altering the intrinsic semantic information contained in the graph-structured data and thus resulting in limited generalizability of GCL-based models.
elements.sent-2411-250: To minimize the risk of altering intrinsic semantic information with strong augmentation, we introduce a conditional distribution learning strategy to guide graph representation learning.

elements.sent-2411-254: Specifically, we present a metric named the distribution divergence to quantify the divergence between these two conditional distributions as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:lossdd} \begin{split} {\mathcal{L}_d} = - \frac{1}{n_g}\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{n_g} {p\left( {\mathbf{h}_i^w|{\mathbf{h}_i}} \right)\log \left( p\left( {\mathbf{h}_i^s|{\mathbf{h}_i}} \right) \right)} \end{split}\end{equation} By minimizing ${\mathcal{L _d}$, the conditional distributions of weakly augmented node embeddings given the original node embeddings are employed to supervise the corresponding conditional distributions of strongly augmented node embeddings given the original node embeddings.}

elements.sent-2411-258: To evaluate the similarity between a positive pair $\left( {\mathbf{h _i^w, {\mathbf{h _i \right)$, the similarity loss is formulated as follows: \begin{equation}\label{eq:losssim} \begin{split} {\mathcal{L}_s} = - \frac{1}{{{n_g}}}\sum\limits_{i = 1}^{{n_g}} {\log \frac{{\exp \left( {sim\left( {\mathbf{h}_i^w,{\mathbf{h}_i}} \right)} \right)}}{{\sum\limits_{j \ne i}^{n_g} {\exp \left( {sim\left( {\mathbf{h}_i^w,{\mathbf{h}_j}} \right)} \right)} }}}. \end{split}\end{equation} Considering a pair of negative node embeddings $\left( {\mathbf{h _i^w,{\mathbf{h _j \right)$ $\left(j \ne i\right)$ in Eq. \eqref{eq:losssim , $\mathbf{h _i^w$ does not participate in the message-passing of the CDL model involving with ${\mathbf{h _j}$, as ${\mathbf{h _j}$ originates from the original view.}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-260: In this section, we introduce a semisupervised training scheme for graph classification, which consists of two stages: a pretraining stage and a fine-tuning stage.
elements.sent-2411-261: The pretraining framework of the CDL model includes a shared GNN encoder module of graph-structured data and a projection head module.
elements.sent-2411-262: In the shared GNN encoder module, we perform graph representation learning according to the graph classification techniques.
elements.sent-2411-263: To improve the generalizability of the CDL model, we apply a batch normalization function in individual layers \cite{Ioffe2015BN .}
elements.sent-2411-264: We further employ the projection head module \cite{Chen2020CL , which consists of a two-layer MLP, to obtain the projections of $\mathbf{H}$ and $\mathbf{H _w$ for contrastive learning, i.e., \begin{equation}\label{eq:proj} \begin{split} \mathbf{P} = {\mathbf{W}^{\left( 2 \right)}}\sigma\left( {{\mathbf{W}^{\left( 1 \right)}}\mathbf{H}} \right) \mathbf{P}_w = {\mathbf{W}^{\left( 2 \right)}}\sigma\left( {{\mathbf{W}^{\left( 1 \right)}}\mathbf{H}_w} \right). \end{split}\end{equation} The pretraining framework of the CDL model aims to ensure the consistency of the intrinsic semantic information between a weakly augmented view and the original graph-structured data.}}
elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Optimization Procedure for CDL} \label{alg:sscdl} \begin{algorithmic} \STATE { Input:} The training graphs $\mathcal{G}_r = \left\{ {\left( {\mathcal{G}_i} \right)} \right\}_{i = 1}^{{n_g}}$, the testing graphs $\mathcal{G}_t = \left\{ {\left( {\mathcal{G}_i} \right)} \right\}_{i = 1}^{{n_t}}$, the number of iterations $epochs$, parameters $\alpha > 0 $ and $\beta > 0$. \STATE Construct a weakly augmented view $\mathcal{G}_s$ and a strongly augmented view $\mathcal{G}_w$ from $\mathcal{G}_r$; \FOR{ ${t = 1}$ to $epochs$ } \STATE Compute ${\mathbf{P}}$ and ${\mathbf{P}_w}$ via Eq. \eqref{eq:proj} using the unlabeled parts of $\mathcal{G}_r$ and $\mathcal{G}_w$, respectively; \STATE Update the network by minimizing ${\mathcal{L}_{s}}$ in Eq. \eqref{eq:losssim} \ENDFOR \FOR{ ${t = 1}$ to $epochs$ } \STATE Compute $\mathbf{p}_i$ via Eq. \eqref{eq:sm} for each labeled graph in $\mathcal{G}_r$; \STATE Compute ${\mathbf{P}}$ and ${\mathbf{P}_w}$ via Eq. \eqref{eq:proj} using the labeled parts of $\mathcal{G}_r$ and $\mathcal{G}_w$, respectively; \STATE Compute $p\left( {\mathbf{h}_i^w|{\mathbf{h}_i}} \right)$ and $p\left( {\mathbf{h}_i^s|{\mathbf{h}_i}} \right)$ via Eq. \eqref{eq:ff} using the labeled parts of $\mathcal{G}_r$, $\mathcal{G}_s$ and $\mathcal{G}_w$; \STATE Update the network by minimizing ${\mathcal{L}}$ in Eq. \eqref{eq:ovloss} \ENDFOR \STATE Compute $\mathbf{p}_i$ via Eq. \eqref{eq:sm} for each testing graph in $\mathcal{G}_t$; \STATE { Output:} The predicted labels of the testing graphs $\mathcal{G}_t$. \end{algorithmic}

elements.sent-2411-298: In this paper, we introduce the CDL method for semisupervised graph classification tasks.
elements.sent-2411-299: The CDL model is designed to align the conditional distributions of weakly and strongly augmented features over the original features.
elements.sent-2411-300: The proposed CDL method leverages the diversity and abundance provided by graph-structured data augmentations while preserving intrinsic semantic information.
elements.sent-2411-301: Moreover, CDL resolves the conflict between the message-passing mechanism of GNNs and the contrastive learning of negative pairs within intraviews.
elements.sent-2411-302: Extensive experimental results with various graph datasets demonstrate the superior performance of the CDL method.
